RoboticsSpatial descriptions and transformationsDescriptionsPositionOrientationFrameMappingsTranslated framesRotated framesGeneral framesTransformationsOperatorsTranslational operatorsRotational operatorsTransformation operatorsTransformation arithmeticCompound transformationsInverting a transformTransform equationsThree angle rotationRPY angleZ-Y-X Euler angleZ-Y-Z Euler angleEquivalent angle-axis representationEuler parameterTransformation of free vectors

Robotics

Robotics are split into 4 major subdisciplines: mechanical manipulation, locomotion, computer vision, and artificial intelligence.

Spatial descriptions and transformations

In order to describe the position and orientation of a body in space, we attach a coordinate system, or frame, rigidly to the object. We often think of transforming the description of these attributes of a body from one frame to another.

Descriptions

Position

AP represents a position vector written in coordinate system {A}.

AP=[pxpypz]
Orientation

In order to describe the orientation of a body, we will attach a coordinate system {B} to the body. Unit vectors XB , YB and ZB are the principal directions of coordinate system {B}.

RB=[XBYBZB]

ARB is a rotation matrix that describes {B} relative to {A}.

ARB=[AXBAYBAZB]
RA⋅ARB=RB

The components of any vector are simply the projections of that vector onto the unit directions of its reference frame. Hence, each component of ARB can be written as the dot product of a pair of unit vectors.

ARB=RA−1⋅RB=[XATYATZAT][XBYBZB]=[XB⋅XAYB⋅XAZB⋅XAXB⋅YAYB⋅YAZB⋅YAXB⋅ZAYB⋅ZAZB⋅ZA]

The dot product of two unit vectors yields the cosine of the angle between them, so the elements on ARB are called direction cosine.

The rows of the matrix ARB are the unit vectors of {A} expressed in {B}.

ARBT=ARB=[BXATBYATBZAT]

This suggests that the inverse of a rotation matrix is equal to its transpose.

ARBT⋅ARB=[AXBTAYBTAZBT][AXBAYBAZB]=I3

From linear algebra, the inverse of a matrix with orthonormal columns is equal to its transpose.

ARB=BRA−1=ARBT
Frame

Frame is a set of 4 vectors giving position and orientation information, used as a description of one coordinate system relative to another. One vector locates the position and 3 more describe its orientation.

The description of a frame can be thought of as a position vector and a rotation matrix. A frame is a coordinate system where we give the orientation amd a position vector which locates its origin relative to some other embedding frame.

Positions could be represented by a frame whose rotation-matrix part is the identity matrix and whose position-vector part locates the point being described. Likewise, an orientation could be represented by a frame whose position-vector part was the zero vector.

For example, frame is described by ARB and APBORG where APBORG is the vector that locates the origin of the frame {B}.

{B}={ARB,APBORG}

Mappings

Mapping means changing descriptions from fame to frame.

Translated frames

Only in the special case of equivalent orientations may we add vectors that are defined in terms of different frames.

AP=BP+APBORG
Rotated frames

The columns of a rotation matrix ARB all have unit magnitude, and are orthogonal. Therefore, the columns of ARB represent the unit direction vectors of frame {B} expressed in frame {A}. The rows of ARB ( .i.e the columns of ARBT ) represent the direction cosines of frame {A} expressed in frame {B}.

In the case of the coincident origins for two frames, we note that the components of position vector AP is the projection of AP onto the unit directions of its frame {A}.

AP=ARB⋅BP=[AXB⋅BPAYB⋅BPAZB⋅BP]=[ApxApyApz]
General frames

A general transformation mapping of a vector from its description in one frame to a description in a second frame.

AP=ARB⋅BP+APBORG

We define a 4 x 4 matrix operator and use 4 x 1 position vectors:

AP=ATB⋅BP
[AP1]=[ARBAPBORG01][BP1]=[ARBBP+APBORG1]

The 4 x 4 matrix ATB is called a homogeneous transform. For our purposes, it can be regarded as a construction used to cast the rotation and translation of the general transform into a single matrix form.

The description of frame {B} relative to {A} is ATB.

Transformations

Operators

Translational operators

A vector AP1 is translated by a vector AQ. The result of the operation is a new vector AP2, calculated as

AP2=AP1+AQ
AP2=DQ(q)AP1

where q is the signed magnitude of the translation along the vector direction Q. The DQ operator may be thought of as a homogeneous transform of a special simple form:

DQ(q)=[100qx010qy001qz0001]
Rotational operators

The rotation matrix that rotates vectors through some rotation, R , is the same as the rotation matrix that describes a frame rotated by R relative to the reference frame. We define notation RK(θ) for a rotational operator.

AP2=RK(θ)AP1
Rk1(α)⋅Rk2(β)≠Rk1(α)⋅Rk2(β)except k1=k2

RK(θ) performs a rotation about the axis direction K by θ degrees.

Rx(θ)=[10000cosθ−sinθ00sinθcosθ00001]
Ry(θ)=[cosθ0sinθ00100−sinθ0cosθ00001]
Rz(θ)=[cosθ−sinθ00sinθcosθ0000100001]
Transformation operators

The operator T rotates and translates a vector AP1 to compute a new vector:

AP2=TAP1

A transform is usually thought of as being in the form of a homogeneous transform with general rotation-matrix and position-vector parts.

T=[noap0001]=[nxoxaxpxnyoyaypynzozazpz0001]
approach=[axayaz]orientation=[oxoyoz]normal=[nxnynz]
T−1=[nxnynz−p⋅noxoyoz−p⋅oaxayaz−p⋅a0001]

Three interpretations of this homogeneous transform:

  • It is a description of a frame. ATB describes the frame {B} relative to the frame {A}. The columns of ARB are unit vectors defining the directions of the principal axes of {B} , and APBORG locates the position of the origin of {B}.
  • It is a transform mapping. ATB maps BP to AP.
  • It is a transform operator. T operates on AP1 to create AP2.

The frame and transform will both be used to refer to a position vector plus an orientation. Frame refers to pose as a description, and transform implies its function as a mapping or operator.

Transformation arithmetic

Compound transformations

A transformation A by a second transformation B

Postmultiplying of B relative to A applies the transformation with respect to the frame of A.

T=A⋅B

Premultiplying of B relative to A applies the transformation with respect to the base coordinate frame.

T=B⋅A

Frame {C} is relative to frame {B}, and frame {B} is relative to frame (A}. We can transform CP into AP as

AP=ATBBTCCP
ATC=[ARBBRCARBBPCORG+APBORG01]
Inverting a transform

To find BTA, we must compute BRA and BPAORG from ARB and APBORG

BRA=ARBT

Change the description of APBORG into {B}:

B(APBORG)=BRAAPBORG+BPAORG=0
BPAORG=−BRAAPBORG=−ARBTAPBORG

Notation BTA:

BTA=[ARBT−ARBTAPBORG01]
BTA=ATB−1

Transform equations

A frame {D} can be expressed as products of transformations in two different ways.

UTD=UTAATD
UTD=UTBBTCCTD

Construct a transform equation:

UTAATD=UTBBTCCTD

Transform equations can be used to solve for transforms in the case of n unknown transforms and n transform equations.

BTC=UTB−1UTAATDCTD−1

If an arrow points the opposite way in a chain of transforms, we simply compute its inverse first.

UTD=UTBBTCCTD
UTD=UTBCTB−1CTD

Three angle rotation

RPY angle

Start with the frame coincident with a known frame {A}. Rotate {B} first about XA by an angle γ, then about YA by an angle β , and about ZA by an angle α. Each of the 3 rotations takes place about an axis in the fixed reference frame {A}.

This convention is referred to as X-Y-Z fixed angles, or roll, pitch, yaw angles.

ARBXYZ(γ,β,α)=RZ(α)RY(β)RX(γ)=[cosα−sinα0sinαcosα0001][cosβ0sinβ010−sinβ0cosβ][1000cosγ−sinγ0sinγcosγ]
ARBXYZ(γ,β,α)=[cosαcosβcosαsinβsinγ−sinαcosγcosαsinβcosγ+sinαsinγsinαcosβsinαsinβsinγ+cosαcosγsinαsinβcosγ−cosαsinγ−sinβcosβsinγcosβcosγ]

 

Z-Y-X Euler angle

Start with the frame coincident with a known frame {A}. Rotate {B} first about ZB by an angle α, then about YB by an angle β , and about XB by an angle γ.

In this representation, each rotation is performed about an axis of the moving system {B} rather than one of the fixed reference {A}.

ARBZ′Y′X′(α,β,γ)=RZ(α)RY(β)RX(γ)=[cosα−sinα0sinαcosα0001][cosβ0sinβ010−sinβ0cosβ][1000cosγ−sinγ0sinγcosγ]
ARBZ′Y′X′(α,β,γ)=[cosαcosβcosαsinβsinγ−sinαcosγcosαsinβcosγ+sinαsinγsinαcosβsinαsinβsinγ+cosαcosγsinαsinβcosγ−cosαsinγ−sinβcosβsinγcosβcosγ]

Three rotations taken about fixed axes yield the same final orientation as the same three rotations taken in opposite order about the axes of the moving frame.

ARBZ′Y′X′(α,β,γ)=[r11r12r13r21r22r23r31r32r33]
β=atan2(−r31,r112+r212)
α=atan2(r21/cosβ,r11/cosβ)
γ=atan2(r32/cosβ,r33/cosβ)

where atan2(y,x) is a two-argument arc tangent function:

atan2(y,x)={arctan⁡(yx)x>0arctan⁡(yx)+πy≥0,x<0arctan⁡(yx)−πy<0,x<0+π2y>0,x=0−π2y<0,x=0undefinedy=0,x=0

atan2(y,x) computes arctan but uses the signs of both x and y to identify the quadrant in which the resulting angle lies.

Z-Y-Z Euler angle

Start with the frame coincident with a known frame {A}. Rotate {B} first about ZB by an angle α, then about YB by an angle β , and about Xb by an angle γ.

ARBZ′Y′Z′(α,β,γ)=[cosαcosβcosγ−sinαsinγ−cosαcosβsinγ−sinαcosγcosαsinβsinαcosβcosγ+cosαsinγ−sinαcosβsinγ+cosαcosγsinαsinβ−sinβcosγsinβsinγcosβ]
ARBZ′Y′Z′(α,β,γ)=[r11r12r13r21r22r23r31r32r33]
β=atan2(r312+r322,r33)
α=atan2(r23/sinβ,r13/sinβ)
γ=atan2(r32/sinβ,−r31/sinβ)
Equivalent angle-axis representation

Given that the rotation matrix relative to the frame {A} is C. Vector K is the z axis direction vector of frame {C}, and an arbitrary vector in frame {A}.

C=[nxoxaxnyoyaynzozaz]
K=[kxkykz]=[axayaz]

Given the frame B, then we find a frame X, s.t.

B=CX
X=C−1B

Rotating B about K is equivalent to rotating X about the z axis of frame {C}.

RK(θ)B=CRz(θ)X=CRz(θ)C−1B
RK(θ)=CRz(θ)C−1
RK(θ)=[nxoxaxnyoyaynzozaz][cosθ−sinθ0sinθcosθ0001][nxnynzoxoyozaxayaz]

Unit direction vector K is called the equivalent axis of a finite rotation. A general orientation of {B} relative to {A} may be written as ARB(K,θ) or RK(θ), and will be called the equivalent angle-axis representation.

The equivalent rotation matrix is

RK(θ)=[kxkxversθ+cosθkxkyversθ−kzsinθkxkzversθ+kysinθkxkyversθ+kzsinθkykyversθ+cosθkykzversθ−kxsinθkxkzversθ−kysinθkykzversθ+kxsinθkzkzversθ+cosθ]

where versθ=1−cosθ. The sign of θ is determined by the right-hand rule, with the thumb pointing along the positive sense of AK.

The equivalent rotation matrix converts from angle-axis representation to rotation-matrix representation.

θ=arccos(r11+r22+r33−12)
K=12sinθ[r32−r23r13−r31r21−r12]
Euler parameter

Another representation of orientation is by means of four numbers called the Euler parameters. In terms of the equivalent axis K=[kxkykz]T and the equivalent angle θ, the Euler parameters are given by

ϵ1=kxsinθ2
ϵ2=kysinθ2
ϵ3=kzsinθ2
ϵ4=cosθ2

These four quantities are not independent

ϵ12+ϵ22+ϵ32+ϵ42=1

An orientation can be visualized as a point on a unit hypersphere in four-dimensional space.

Sometimes, the Euler parameters are viewed as a 3 x 1 vector plus a scalar. However, as a 4 x 1 vector, the Euler parameters are known as a unit quaternion.

The rotation matrix Rϵ that is equivalent to a set of Euler parameters is

Rϵ=[1−2ϵ22−2ϵ322(ϵ1ϵ2−ϵ3ϵ4)2(ϵ1ϵ3+ϵ2ϵ4)2(ϵ1ϵ2+ϵ3ϵ4)1−2ϵ12−2ϵ322(ϵ2ϵ3−ϵ1ϵ4)2(ϵ1ϵ3−ϵ2ϵ4)2(ϵ2ϵ3+ϵ1ϵ4)1−2ϵ12−2ϵ22]

Given a rotation matrix, the equivalent Euler parameters are

ϵ1=r32−r234ϵ4
ϵ2=r13−r314ϵ4
ϵ3=r21−r124ϵ4
ϵ4=121+r11+r22+r33

Transformation of free vectors

Two vectors are equal if they have the same dimensions, magnitude, and direction.

The line vector refers to a vector that is dependent on its line of action, along with direction and magnitude, for causing its effects. The effects of a force vector depend upon its line of action (or point of application), so it would then be considered a line vector.

A free vector refers to a vector that may be positioned anywhere in space without loss or change of meaning, provided that magnitude and direction are preserved.

In the case of a free vector, all counts is the magnitude and direction. Only the rotation matrix relating the two systems is used in transforming. The relative locations of the origins do not enter into the calculation.

For example, a pure moment vector is always a free vector. If we have a moment vector BN that is known in terms of {B}, then we calculate the same moment in terms of frame {A} as

AN=ARBBN

The velocity vector is a free vector. A velocity vector written in {B}, BV, is written in {A} as

AV=ARBBV
